narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. As a male student from an upper-middle-income family, planning to attend full-time, with a solid LSAT score of 37 and an impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.70, you stand out in some positive ways compared to typical applicants. However, your school decile rankings—placing you in the bottom 10% for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades—are significantly lower than the average student's mid-range performance, and these stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward predicting bar failure. Your Year 3 decile had the single biggest influence, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, meaning your earlier academic struggles in school weighed most heavily in this decision. Next in line was your LSAT score, which, while slightly above average, still nudged the prediction toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps because it didn't fully offset those low rankings. Your family income bracket contributed moderately, along with your racial background as White—which is common in the dataset but didn't help enough here—then your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status, all playing smaller roles in tipping the scales toward rejection. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to peers who typically have stronger in-school deciles around the 5th or 6th level, despite your strengths in LSAT and GPA that exceed dataset averages. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag that past school performance strongly correlates with bar outcomes in our analysis. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT if possible to push it higher, build proven study habits through targeted prep courses that mimic law school rigor, and seek mentorship to address whatever held back your school deciles, like time management or subject mastery. Demonstrating improvement in a post-baccalaureate program or bar prep simulation could help rebuild your profile for another application. You're already ahead with your GPA and LSAT—channel that drive, and with deliberate steps, you can shift the odds in your favor next time. Hang in there; many students turn predictions like this around through persistence.
